source_paper_title: Improved Bounds for Single-Nomination Impartial Selection
context:
Page 14, Section 5 (Upper Bounds), end of introductory paragraph before Theorem 5.1

"Like the bounds of Fischer and Klimm they approach 3/4 as n → ∞ and thus do not rule out the existence of an impartial mechanism that is close to 3/4-optimal when n is large. Whether such a mechanism exists is an intriguing open question, the resolution of which is likely to require new insights."
title: Existence of an impartial mechanism approaching 3/4-optimality for large n in the single-nomination model
latex:
Consider the single-nomination impartial selection setting.

- For each integer \(n\ge 2\), let \(G_n\) be the set of directed graphs \(G=(V,E)\) with \(V=\{1,\dots,n\}\), no self-loops, and outdegree exactly \(1\) for every vertex (each agent casts exactly one nomination).
- A (randomized) selection mechanism is a map \(f\) that assigns to each \(G\in G_n\) a probability distribution \(f(G)\in[0,1]^n\) over vertices with \(\sum_{v\in V} f_v(G)=1\).
- The mechanism is impartial if for every \(G,G'\in G_n\) and every \(v\in V\), whenever \(G\) and \(G'\) differ only in \(v\)'s outgoing edge (equivalently, \(G\setminus(\{v\}\times V)=G'\setminus(\{v\}\times V)\)), we have \(f_v(G)=f_v(G')\).
- Let \(\delta^-(v,G)\) denote the indegree of \(v\) in \(G\), and \(\Delta(G)=\max_{u\in V}\delta^-(u,G)\) the maximum indegree.
- The approximation ratio (optimality guarantee) of \(f\) on \(G_n\) is
\[
\alpha(f,n)=\inf_{G\in G_n:\,\Delta(G)>0}\frac{\sum_{v\in V} f_v(G)\,\delta^-(v,G)}{\Delta(G)}.
\]

Problem: Determine whether the supremum of \(\alpha(f,n)\) over all impartial mechanisms \(f\) tends to \(3/4\) as \(n\to\infty\). Equivalently, decide whether for every \(\varepsilon>0\) there exists \(n_0\) and an impartial mechanism \(f\) such that for all \(n\ge n_0\),
\[
\alpha(f,n)\ge \frac{3}{4}-\varepsilon.
\]
(Or, alternatively, prove that no sequence of impartial mechanisms can achieve \(\alpha(f,n)\to 3/4\).)
location_in_paper: Page 14, Section 5 (Upper Bounds), end of introductory paragraph before Theorem 5.1
open_problem_quote: "Like the bounds of Fischer and Klimm they approach 3/4 as n → ∞ and thus do not rule out the existence of an impartial mechanism that is close to 3/4-optimal when n is large. Whether such a mechanism exists is an intriguing open question, the resolution of which is likely to require new insights."
